data.title: Germany's finance minister Klingbeil says Washington meeting addressed rare earth price floor
data.description:
Key points:
  • G7+ discusses price floors for rare earths to cut China's market influence
  • Klingbeil emphasizes cooperation, not confrontation, with China
  • Foreign ministers to meet soon on rare earths strategy

By Maria Martinez

Finance ministers from a group of advanced economies meeting in Washington on Monday discussed a possible price floor as well as partnerships to secure strategic rare-earth supplies, Germany's Lars Klingbeil said, adding that many issues remained open.

"There are a whole series of questions that remain open and that are to be clarified in the coming weeks —not only among finance ministers, but also among foreign ministers and energy ministers," Klingbeil said.

He said discussion had just started but the issue would be a central topic under the French presidency of the Group of Seven advanced economies this year.

The meeting in Washington brought finance ministers from the G7 as well as their counterparts from Australia, Mexico, South Korea and India. Klingbeil said a meeting of foreign ministers to address rare earths will take place soon.

PRICE FLOORS FOR RARE EARTHS ON THE TABLE

The finance ministers discussed price floors for rare earths, a minimum price ensuring producers outside of China receive at least that level even if market prices fall due to China's dominance and ability to flood markets with supply.

"This is not about working against anyone, but about strengthening cooperation among partners," Klingbeil said.

There has been no reaction from China to the meeting, he said, while stressing that Germany's strategy towards China is de-risking, not decoupling.

"The advantage of minimum prices is indeed that the market then knows what prices it can expect, and that we can minimize the influence of countries that may then try to influence market prices," Klingbeil said.

Nevertheless, he said countries need to weigh carefully the consequences that price floors could have in the coming years.

"The discussion has now begun and we want to bring it to a quick conclusion," Klingbeil said.

data.title: Inflation Expected to Cool After Initial Data Adjustment — Market Talk
data.description: Inflation indicators could move up in the short term as a lasting impact of last year's data shortage, but the long-term outlook remains stable due, mainly, to lukewarm wage growth, SignatureFD's Tony Welch says. He doesn't have a forecast for tomorrow's CPI report. Economists surveyed by WSJ expect both the 12-month headline and core readings to print 2.7%, keeping inflation above the Fed's 2% target. "This does not look like a long-term inflationary backdrop to us," Welch says. "Wage growth just is not strong enough to support breakaway inflation out there." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: US-listed shares of Baidu rise; Benchmark lifts PT
data.description:
** U.S.-listed shares of Chinese tech giant Baidu rise 6.2% to $152.45

** Brokerage Benchmark raises PT on BIDU to $215 from $158, implying ~50% upside to stock's last close

** Benchmark views the upcoming Kunlun spinoff as a major catalyst; says Kunlun is well-positioned to broaden its customer base across industries

** Brokerage says BIDU's Kunlun chips add an additional layer of pricing flexibility and competitiveness to its cloud offerings

** "Baidu’s integrated chips + cloud strategy provides further potential for margin upside versus pure-play cloud providers," Benchmark says

** Benchmark says BIDU's robotaxi service, Apollo Go, continues to narrow gap with Alphabet-owned Waymo, with weekly fully driverless rides reaching about 250k as of October 2025

** 25 of 33 brokerages recommend rating of "buy" or higher, 7 "hold" and 1 "sell"; stock has a median PT of $159, according to LSEG-compiled data

** BIDU gained ~55% in 2025

data.title: Meta Needs So Much Capital It Hired Its Own Banker — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Levine

Meta Platforms announced on Monday that Dina Powell McCormick, who comes from the worlds of international finance and politics, has become the company's president and vice chairman. The move signals Meta won't slow its enormous artificial-intelligence data center buildout soon.

As a child, McCormick came to America as an Egyptian immigrant who spoke no English. She has been involved in politics, serving in both the George W. Bush and Donald Trump administrations in national security roles. She also worked at Goldman Sachs for 16 years, largely in senior leadership. Most recently, she was president and vice chairman of merchant bank BDT & MSD Partners.

JPMorgan Chase CEO Jaime Dimon praised McCormick last year when she was named one of the 100 Most Influential Women in U.S. Finance by Barron's. "She is a better banker and has more or better relationships than many big finance CEOs. She's the full package — doing it all at the most senior levels."

McCormick was named to the Meta board last year, but left in December.

"Dina's experience at the highest levels of global finance, combined with her deep relationships around the world, makes her uniquely suited to help Meta manage this next phase of growth," Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg said.

Meta stands out among the b ig artificial intelligence data center spenders because it doesn't have a cloud where it rents out servers to others. It spent about $70 billion last year on AI data centers, all for its own use, and hasn't indicated that spending will slow down.

The buildout was financed by Meta's substantial cash flows at first, but the accelerating pace of capital expenditures sent Meta to financing markets. Over several bond offerings, Meta sold $40 billion in debt in 2024 and 2025, reshaping its once-pristine balance sheet. Last year, it also entered into a $27 billion joint venture to fund its massive Louisiana data center complex. Meta only owns 20% of the joint venture, so it isn't on the company's balance sheet.

The push by many to build out AI data centers has butted up against limits since the beginning. Nvidia chips were in short supply; in 2026, memory and storage chips will see depleted inventories. Data centers, which have high power requirements, have had to contend with an aging electricity grid and slow regulatory processes that can't keep up.

Meta hiring its own banker with international relations experience may indicate that the company is charging ahead with its AI capital plans, and that financing is becoming another bottleneck it will have to break through.

Write to Adam Levine at adam.levine@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Gold and Silver Set New Records on Fed Probe — Market Talk
data.description: Gold and silver futures settled at new records in reaction to the DOJ criminal investigation into Jerome Powell — which sparked a retort from the Fed Chair that set the stage for a prolonged battle over the future of the board and interest rates. This and other sources of volatility from the Trump administration sent traders deeper into precious metals. "The size and speed of the rally in gold and silver is an eye opener, leaving many seasoned veterans of the metals markets wondering how far the rally can go," says Robert Yawger of Mizuho Securities USA in a note. Front-month gold finishes up 2.5% to $4,604.30 a troy ounce, and silver settles up 7.3% to $84.61 a troy ounce. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)
